chunks.page_summary: This page details the integration of Rheonics sensors using Modbus TCP with B&R X20 PLCs and B&R Automation Studio, including prerequisites and connection diagrams.
chunks.extracted_text:
Phone: +1 713 234 0863

Modbus TCP integration with B&R X20 and B&R Automation studio
Modified on: Sun, 15 Feb, 2026 at 12:50 AM

Modbus TCP communication setup with a PLC
Rheonics Sensors support Modbus RTU and TCP. For Modbus TCP, ethernet connection is required. The example below shows the connection steps and configuration of Modbus registers on a PLC. This example uses B&R X20 PLC and B&R Automation studio to configure the communication.

Prerequisite:
A PLC which supports Modbus TCP either natively or through an expansion module
Rheonics Sensor Electronics (SME) with Ethernet and Modbus TCP enabled
Ethernet on SME should be set to either DHCP or Static IP through RCP
IP address of the Rheonics SME - this can be read out from the SME using RCP software

Ethernet Connection
The sensor electronics (SME) can be connected directly to the B&R control with an RJ45 Ethernet cable. Alternatively, a router, switch or hub can be used to connect the sensor and PLC to the factory LAN.
key_facts: Rheonics Sensors support both Modbus RTU and Modbus TCP protocols.
Modbus TCP communication requires an Ethernet connection.
This document provides an example of Modbus TCP integration using a B&R X20 PLC and B&R Automation studio.
A prerequisite for Modbus TCP integration is a PLC that supports Modbus TCP either natively or via an expansion module.
Rheonics Sensor Electronics (SME) must have both Ethernet and Modbus TCP enabled.
The Ethernet configuration on the SME should be set to either DHCP or Static IP, adjustable through RCP.
The IP address of the Rheonics SME can be obtained using RCP software.
The SME can be connected directly to the B&R control using an RJ45 Ethernet cable.
Alternatively, a router, switch, or hub can be used to connect the sensor and PLC to the factory LAN.

chunks.page_summary: This page provides a step-by-step guide for configuring Modbus TCP communication between sensor electronics and a B&R PLC using B&R Automation Studio, focusing on network interface and Modbus parameters.
chunks.extracted_text:
PLC configuration steps for establishing Modbus communication
This project uses B&R Automation Studio to setup Modbus TCP communication between the Sensor electronics and a B&R PLC. A project has already been created in Automation Studio and the corresponding PLC from B&R has been added.

Interface configuration
The numbers in front of the text are highlighted in front of the steps performed.

1, 2 Select Physical View and select the desired interface for the Modbus configuration.
3 Open Configuration of the interface.
4 Select Mode: enter IP address manually. (For direct connection of the sensor to the PLC) Enter an IP address with a suitable subnet mask for the interface.
5 Activate Modbus communication = on

Addition of TCP_any interface
1 Select the interface in the Physical View.
key_facts: B&R Automation Studio is used to configure Modbus TCP communication.
Modbus TCP communication is established between Sensor electronics and a B&R PLC.
A pre-existing project in Automation Studio with the B&R PLC added is required.
The configuration process involves selecting the 'Physical View' and the desired interface (ETH/IF2).
Manual IP address entry (192.168.0.100) and subnet mask (255.255.255.0) are required for direct sensor-to-PLC connection.
Modbus communication must be explicitly activated within the Modbus parameters.
The device X20CP1585 is configured with version 1.8.
diagram_labels: 4 (Mode section with IP address and Subnet Mask)

key_facts: The document details the process of adding and configuring a Modbus TCP/IP communication module for a sensor within the B&R Automation Studio software environment.
Users must locate 'Modbus' in the Toolbox and then add the 'Modbus Tcp_any' component.
The 'Modbus Tcp_any' is identified as a 'Generic Modbus Station' in the hardware catalog.
The configuration steps require specifying an IP address for the sensor, ensuring it matches the interface's range, and setting the TCP Port to 502.

The configuration includes parameters for Ethernet mode, IP address, unit identifier, TCP port, and timeouts.
The example configuration for Modbus Tcp_any aligns with guidance from the Rheonics Modbus-Manual.
Block 1 in the Tcp_any configuration should be defined based on the Rheonics Modbus-Manual, which covers commands and use.
The Rheonics Modbus-Manual is accessible via the URL: https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/articles/81000297668.

chunks.page_summary: This page provides a guide for configuring Modbus TCP communication to read sensor data, specifically viscosity measurements and status, from Rheonics sensors into a PLC.

software_configuration_ui.menu_bar: File Edit View Open Project Debug Source Control Online Tools Window Help

modbus_manual_excerpt_1.heading: Reading Multiple parameters; Viscosity Average value in float, UInt and status.

modbus_parameters.address: 64 = 0x0040 (word) (Address in Hex of the first register)

extracted_text.caption_1: Define channels according to the Rheonics Modbus-Manual - Input Registers (https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/articles/81000297664) - specify data to be read

parameter_1.description: Parameter 1 value scaled by 100 as signed integer

parameter_2.description: Parameter 2 value scaled by 100 as signed integer

parameter_3.description: Parameter 3 value scaled by 100 as signed integer

parameter_4.description: Parameter 4 value scaled by 100 as signed integer

extracted_text.caption_2: Repeat steps for all the measurement parameters from the sensor you want to access from your PLC.

modbus_downloads_section.heading: Modbus related downloads & links
modbus_downloads_section.paragraph: The following is a list of modbus manuals, register tables, and configuration files that are provided here to simplify the integration of Rheonics sensors with a PLC when using Modbus TCP.

key_facts: This document guides users through configuring Modbus TCP communication to read measurement parameters from Rheonics sensors using a PLC.
It demonstrates setting up Modbus communication parameters such as IP address (192.168.0.0), TCP port (502), Unit ID (255), and using function code FC4 to read input registers.
The configuration example illustrates reading Viscosity Average as a Float32 (e.g., 30.54 cP) and as a scaled UInt16 (e.g., 3054 cP), along with a status (UInt16 value of 0).
Rheonics provides Modbus manuals, register tables, and configuration files to simplify the integration of their sensors with PLCs using Modbus TCP.
The process involves defining channels for specific data types such as REAL for Float32, INT for Int16, and UINT for UInt16 parameters.
diagram_labels: Reading Multiple parameters; Viscosity Average value in float, UInt and status.
Parameter 1 value scaled by 100 as signed integer
Parameter 2 value scaled by 100 as signed integer
Parameter 3 value scaled by 100 as signed integer
Parameter 4 value scaled by 100 as signed integer

chunks.page_summary: This page provides an overview of Rheonics Modbus TCP communication, including register types, configuration files for Modbuspoll software, and troubleshooting guides for common issues like Ethernet connectivity, IP address management, and Modbus register checking.
chunks.extracted_text:
Rheonics Modbus TCP Manual
(https://rheonics.com/resources/download-request-manual-modbus-tcp/)
Rheonics Modbus registers support articles
*   input (https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/articles/81000297664) registers
*   data (https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/articles/81000297665) types
*   command (https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/articles/81000297668) types and response
*   error (https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/articles/81000297667) status

Modbuspoll configuration file for input registers [download link, for use with modbuspoll software]
*   input registers
*   extended input registers (externally writable modbus register and estimated parameters)
*   4-20mA configuration registers
*   Location configuration registers
*   Ethernet configuration registers
*   Sensor s/n, Hardware and Firmware version registers

Troubleshooting

How to connect ethernet cable to Rheonics electronics?
See the support knowledge article on connecting ethernet cable to sensor electronics (https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/articles/81000300292).

How to get the IP address of the Rheonics Sensor connected to the LAN?
Using RCP software (https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/articles/81000297177), you can both check the assigned IP address and also assign a static IP address or switch the unit to DHCP.

How to check Modbus registers on the Rheonics sensor using PC?
Users can use Modbus polling software to check the modbus registers on the Rheonics sensor using a PC. Modbuspoll (https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/articles/81000388169), is an external software that can be used.
[We do not imply any guarantee or undertake any liability for your use of the software, use of any third party software is up to the customer]

Rheonics sensor electronics SMET does not get an IP address
Check that the sensor was ordered with ethernet and Modbus TCP option.
Check the ethernet setting on the network where the sensor is connected.
If a direct connection is used to PLC then ensure the sensor is assigned an IP address and subnet as needed by the PLC. Refer to the (https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/articles/81000297604) Modbus TCP support article (https://support.rheonics.com/en/support/solutions/articles/81000297604) to configure static IP
key_facts: Rheonics Modbus registers are categorized into input, data, command, and error types, each with dedicated support articles for detailed information.
A Modbuspoll configuration file is provided for input registers, intended for use with Modbuspoll software.
The Modbuspoll configuration covers various register types including input, extended input, 4-20mA configuration, location, Ethernet, and sensor serial number/hardware/firmware version.
To connect an Ethernet cable to Rheonics electronics, users should consult the support knowledge article on connecting Ethernet cable to sensor electronics.
The RCP software can be utilized to check and assign IP addresses for Rheonics sensors connected to a LAN, or to switch the unit to DHCP.
Modbus polling software, such as Modbuspoll, is recommended for checking Modbus registers on a Rheonics sensor via a PC.
If a Rheonics sensor electronics SMET does not obtain an IP address, verify that the sensor was ordered with the Ethernet and Modbus TCP option and check the Ethernet settings on the network.
For a direct connection to a PLC, ensure the sensor has a static IP address and subnet configured as required by the PLC, referring to the Modbus TCP support article for guidance.
warnings_notes: We do not imply any guarantee or undertake any liability for your use of the software, use of any third party software is up to the customer.

Rheonics Modbus TCP manual (https://rheonics.com/resources/download-request-manual-modbus-tcp/)

HPHT probe density and viscosity meter DVP product page (https://rheonics.com/products/inline-gas-lng-density-meter-dvp/)
HPHT inline flow through density meter and viscometer DVM product page (https://rheonics.com/products/hpht-pvt-density-meter-viscometer-dvm/)

A Modbus TCP manual from Rheonics is available online.
Product manuals for Rheonics SRV, SRD, DVP, and DVM sensors are accessible through a general manuals page.
A product page for the Inline viscometer SRV is available.
A product page for the Inline density and viscosity meter SRD is available.
A product page for the HPHT probe density and viscosity meter DVP is available.
A product page for the HPHT inline flow through density meter and viscometer DVM is available.
